item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another skip from yours I really like, although I agree that the existence of a 200-million year constant civilization plays a bit with my suspension of disbelief. I would have really prefered if the clinical tone was kept towards the end, as the last message kinda… just tells us everything and ends a bit anticlimatically.

Still, a well deserved +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Near the end I thought that the twist was gonna be that this is basically just fish-internet and we found their weird fetish stuff.
I am not sure I would've liked it more like that, but it certainly has more impact than "Its the squids"

I thought about no-voting, but the sheer volume of this, which until then was of consistent enjoyability changes that to a + 1

item.title: Absolutely Brilliant
item.content:encoded:
I'm sure the length and girth of this article might turn some people off of it but I'll say now that it's worth it in my perception. Meta did a brilliant job of mixing an SCP entry with a Tale just based on the content and how they used logs. The story they wanted to tell with their article comes off as very clear and concise without muddling the lines of an SCP article's clinical tone.

The way Meta was able to combine the Foundation's style with Lovecraft's work is great, and the Lovecraftian aspects all came off as unique despite their shared basis. The way the Foundation analyzes and examines the reality of a Lovecraftian-style anomaly feels right at home with the rest of the work on SCP. Honestly the only criticism I could leverage against this article is the lack of photographs, which is such a nitpick.

This is definitely a +1 and I'm definitely gonna create sockpuppet accounts to give it +10 I'm gonna share it to all my friends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the article definitely an upvote, but I don't like the ending. "They were cephalopods." is just really anticlimactic. I think this article would do better as a whole without the final collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting take on a Cthulu style ancient mystery. It's got some issues imo, some clinical tone breaks, an over-reliance on exposition, and a few details that sort of beggar belief (eg the continuous 200 million year civilization). Nonetheless, I enjoyed the ride and the ambition therein. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a fantastic read and it drew me in and did not let go until the end. The ending had a great *click* moment where everything fell into place and the way you told something huge like this soo believable is imply phenomenal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit, the comments from both of you, while completely valid as your opinion, aren't really things I would personally want to change to this. A matter of taste I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m going to second what khaim said, the decline in clinical tone towards the end (or rather, where I faded out, jeez this is long).
It was mainly this:

Foundation efforts to translate this supposed revelation electronically have failed. It is feared that human technology is simply incompatible with the SCP-4246-4 brain wave

That made my interest fade away.

+ show block
– hide block

translate this supposed revelation electronically

Ok, so. Who had the bright idea to translate a hypothetical “revelation” into something electronic in the 80s? What is even meant by “electronically”? You can’t just stab someone’s brain with an iron rod and plot out a “revelation” on a graph (ok, we’re getting there, but if I want to find out about how someone is in the head. But if I want to know that I’ll start with “hey, what are you thinking” and not with “hey, want this iron in your cranium?”)

it’s is feared

By whom? Why is it “feared”. Back in the day when people didn’t have sufficient tech to measure up what their hypothesies described they didn’t “fear” that human technology was insufficient. (Also a tad bit unclinical eh?)

simply incompatible

As stated earlier, started in the wrong end of the spectrum. Human technology is currently “incompatible” with retroactively observing past events (i.e. the Big Bang). But we still have a pretty good idea of what went on. The “simply incompatible” statement feels like a cop out

Ok, so: we can measure the brain wave, but our tech is incompatible. Doesn’t add up to me.

That ^. And how the article is structured a little bit too chronologically (as in, like a tale or a short story. Not in an article kind of way) just leaves me with a -1. I liked it at the start, but it just didn’t work out for me

Edit: have I been a bit unconstructive in what irked me? Feel like I might have

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article hit so many of my sweet-spots. Would upvote again, if I could.

Oh, they say that it's over
and it just had to be
Oh, they say, that it's over
We're lost children of the sea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pacing and quality of writing kept me hooked throughout the article, but for whatever reason the ending twist just didn't do it for me. Novoting because this was a very well constructed article with tons of really admirable attention to detail, but the payoff, for my money, wasn't worth everything that came before it (although I may change my mind after giving it some thought, we'll see.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything Alex Jones said about the extradimensional aliens is correct. It's a breakaway civilization!

But seriously: this is good. I haven't much to say; other than, I'm actually glad the ending is a subversion of sorts. The Tethyans are/were just cephalopods. This reveal would sound almost silly out of context, but here, it works. It's a quaint end to a frankly amazing story, without taking away from its content. I think it actually enriches it — the civilization that excelled in areas we could never dream of was reduced to a lower state, if by choice, because the discoveries they made and the artifacts they built weren't meant for the world.

The build-up throughout the article seemed to point to an alien doomsday about to happen but ended with a reminder of the status quo. And I really like that.

+1 for a story that reels you in and brings you along for the ride.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

This is a fine story. I do not like it as an SCP because it is told as a story. There's some attempt at clinical tone at the beginning but it quickly drops out and the author just tells us things. Part of the SCP format is the idea that these are in-universe documents, and this doesn't even come close.

Honestly, my suggestion is to cut almost everything and rewrite it as a tale. It's already a pretty good short story and you could make it really shine if you stopped trying to force it into the SCP format. Leave the very basic anomaly here as a hook; I'd say just the drowning compulsion, but part of the B plot could work too. (Pun intended.) Then link to tale(s) where you have the rest of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It originally specified various species of cephalopods but I realized that, since the in-universe reader already had access to the data, I could further delay it until the final reveal was literally the final word of the draft. It seemed fitting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually skip over lengthy articles since it's hard for a lot of them to grab my attention, but the way this one flowed kept me hooked (heh) from beginning to end. The clinical and informational tone was perfect in my opinion, as well.

One tiny nitpick: the final letter mentions something in aquariums and whatnot moving in rhythm but it doesn't say what they were, and while I imagine this was done for the reveal at the end, it does serve up some confusion as to what is being referred to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first skip this year to really blow me away in that old fashioned, epic kind of way. It's complex and detailed, but not convoluted. It provides answers, but still leaves you wanting to know more. It's long, but it earns every word. And it's just thrilling and terrifying, even for someone who knows next to nothing about Lovecraftian stuff. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, ahem, poem might be of interest to the author and readers:
https://www.drabblecast.org/2013/12/06/drabblecast-305-testimony-emergency-session-naval-cephalopod-command/

+1, great skip

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fantastic piece and a rare example of making things really quite explicit but using that to the piece's advantage. It's a poster child for proper pacing, as well as a fine example of creating an atmosphere of evolving time through the portrayal of changing attitudes and developing research.

An easy +1 for one of your best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wrote a cephalopod-like skipspecies for the SCP-ES some time ago.

This just came out and it feels older already.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oo this was recently in drafts, nice seeing it completed! I didn’t complete the read cause of the length, but I really like the concept, I’ll have to continue reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This piece does so much of what this site has been lacking lately. It takes a concept/anomaly/whatever and treats it as the Foundation would: cold and head-on. Nothing meta. No -J parading as an anomaly because it's created by POI's/GOI's whose whole thing is making joke anomalies. No interviews that should just be a Tale, wearing an SCP format just to be on the Mainlist.

I'm gonna disagree with this bit here in a general sense. While yes, meta and funny articles are more accepted on the mainlist than they were ten years ago, they've mostly always been a part of the SCP format. If every article were like this (an epic Lovecraftian cosmic mystery), then this would not be an exceptional piece because it would have gotten stale after ten years of the same stuff, no matter the quality.

SCP is a format, and the creative things that authors do with (and sometimes despite) that format is what keeps this site interesting. Its cool to praise stuff for being different, but I can't say I agree with tearing down other valid (and successful) styles of articles to accomplish said praise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this rules its gonna get a million upvotes just watch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially I was going to downvote when the lovecraftian nature came clear, given how often it's been used here and how it's usually not well executed.
But then, those logs….
Oh man, you nailed it. You wrote it, conveyed the tale, as Lovecraft could. No emotion, no bias, mere observation and it works!
This is by far my favorite Lovecraftian skip thus far, you kept me hooked throughout the whole article and ended it in a manner that the story is told and the truth has been revealed. Just like H.P. Lovecraft.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What you read is still technically true. That intelligence is there but I worried that communication would take away from its alieness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember the original version of this SCP (back for the 4000 contest if memory serves) and I actually liked the original ending better, where the Singing Stone was sentient and the loneliest thing in the world since the Tethyeans were long gone, but I'm glad it's back up on the site regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Homer, that's your solution to everything. 'Move under the sea'. It's not going to happen."

"Well not with that attitude."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty sure I'd seen an earlier version of this at some point. It's obvious that there was a ton of care that has since gone into this.
I really, really fucking adore this piece.

This is the best Lovecraftian SCP on this site, and that's saying something. It's a wonderful exploration of what is essentially a huge slice of the Mythos, and so successfully grounded in reality. The references are there, but very oblique - the submarine exploration mutinying a-la The Temple, etc. I will even bet you edited the eyes in the image of Adler to get that "Innsmouth look."

This piece does so much of what this site has been lacking lately. It takes a concept/anomaly/whatever and treats it as the Foundation would: cold and head-on. Nothing meta. No -J parading as an anomaly because it's created by POI's/GOI's whose whole thing is making joke anomalies. No interviews that should just be a Tale, wearing an SCP format just to be on the Mainlist.

I know that other Lovecraftian entries have done this, but I wouldn't have hated if one way the Foundation contained this SCP was by normalizing certain themes of it in popular culture. I know it's been done a bunch, but I feel like this entry deserves that device, because I can easily imagine the frustration of artists trying to express their compulsions while being thwarted by the wash of similar concepts treated as fiction. Those dreams can be dismissed because a lot of current popular culture includes some Lovecraftian elements - as inserted by the Foundation, but also likely there by the pervasiveness of what came before.

Anyway, if I had any ability to do so, I'd feature this article.
Since I don't - plus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was long and I enjoyed every bit. My only regret is having but one upvote for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome to see you posting again meta! This was quite the gem to review, and it was overall a fantastic read.

MURMAIDER MURMAIDER MURMAIDER MURMAIDER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow this is a long but fantastic read. Its got a very lovecraftian vibe that I love, yet still stays within the Foundation's tone

Also, splatoon

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Paul_Sedir.jpg

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article is currently unsourced, and therefore may be a violation of the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). If you can provide the source for the image, then I will determine whether it is cc-compliant or not. If it is, then the image will stay. If it isn't, the image will go.

EDIT: The image has been sourced and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hell yes. This is super long, so I'm adding this to my read later list. Looking forward to when I have time to read huge articles.

item.content:encoded:

GO INTO THE WATER…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FUCK YEAH ITS POSTED

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OCEAN MAN, TAKE ME BY THE HAND, TAKE ME TO THE LAND! DO YOU UNDERSTAND?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Darling it's better
Down where it's wetter
Take it from me!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah as someone who frequently fantasizes about being able to live underwater and spend my time there this freaked me the fuck out.

+1
